content.full_text:
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
P.O. Box 807 • 428 J Street • Sacramento, CA 95812-0807
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

October 12, 2000


Gerald J. Klaas
5109 Melvin Drive
Carmichael, CA 95608

Re:    Your Request for Informal Assistance
       Our File No. I-00-204

Dear Mr. Klaas:

       This letter is in response to your request for advice regarding provisions of the
Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Because your question refers to rules outside the Act,
we provide you with informal assistance.²

QUESTION

       Is my former position at the Air Resources Board, namely Staff Information
Systems Analyst – Specialist, a "policy making" position within the meaning of
Department of General Services Rule 62 ?

CONCLUSION

       The FPPC is without authority to interpret or apply the rules of the Department of
General Services to employment classifications established in the Air Resources Board's
conflict of interest code. To the extent that you seek review of the ARB's decision to
designate your former position in its conflict of interest code, an advice letter cannot be
substituted for the hearing procedure established in Section 87307 for challenges to a
code's provisions. As a result, we are unable to answer your question.


¹ Government Code Sections 81000 – 91015. Commission regulations appear at title 2, Sections
18109-18996, of the California Code of Regulations.
² Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the immunity provided by an opinion or
formal written advice. (Regulation 18239(c).)

File No. I-00-204
Page No. 2

FACTS

In April, 2000, you left your employment at the Air Resources Board ("ARB"),
where you had occupied the position of Staff Information Systems Analyst – Specialist.
This position is designated in the ARB's Conflict of Interest Code as a "professional"
position. During your tenure at the ARB you submitted annual Statements of Economic
Interest, and you filed a final Leaving Office Statement upon your departure. You now
wish to enter into a consulting agreement with the Department of General Services
("DGS") under a California Multiple Awards Schedule ("CMAS") contract.

As you understand DGS Rule 62, you must wait 12 months to enter into such a
contract, if designation in the ARB's Conflict of Interest Code establishes that the
position you formerly held is a "policy making" position. The ARB's Office of Legal
Affairs advises that your position was designated as a position that advised decision
makers or made decisions. You contend that your position was not a "policy making"
position within the meaning of DGS Rule 62.

ANALYSIS

The FPPC administers and interprets the provisions of the Political Reform Act.
It does not have the authority to interpret other bodies of law, or rules of other agencies,
such as DGS Rule 62. We cannot, therefore, advise you on whether or not your former
position was a "policy making" position within the meaning of DGS Rule 62.

The Political Reform Act contains a rule similar to DGS Rule 62, in Section
87406(d)(1), which bars designated employees from certain kinds of employment for a
period of one year after leaving state service. Even when addressing the Act's own post-
employment rules, however, the FPPC cannot through an advice letter resolve a dispute
over another agency's designation of specific positions.

The Act establishes broad guidelines employed by state or local agencies in their
decisions as to which employees should be designated in the agency's conflict of interest
code. (Sections 87300, 87302, 87209; Regulation 18730.) These agency-level decisions
are not made by the FPPC, but by the individual agencies as they draft and revise their
particular codes. When the FPPC acts as the code-reviewing body, it necessarily relies
on the agency's specialized knowledge to determine which agency positions should be
designated in its conflict of interest code.

A designated employee who believes that his or her position should not have been
designated may approach the agency under Section 87307 to seek an amendment to the
code, and the employee may appeal to the code reviewing body if the amendment is
denied at the agency level. Such an appeal permits the FPPC to hold a hearing and to
consider relevant facts and argument marshaled by both sides before determining what
the Act requires under the circumstances. FPPC staff cannot circumvent this fact-finding
process by issuing an advice letter on the propriety of a designation in a current, validly

File No. I-00-204
Page No. 3

adopted conflict of interest code. Thus once a code has been promulgated, reviewed, and
approved by the FPPC, we presume (absent an amendment) that a designated position has
been designated because it entails decisionmaking responsibilities that could give rise to
conflicts of interest. (Section 82019.) A position may give rise to a conflict of interest
when the employee is a public official authorized to make, participate in making, or who
may use his official position to influence, governmental decisions. (Section 87100.) We
cannot offer you any advice on the extent to which the DGS regards such positions as
"policy making" positions for purposes of its Rule 62.

We recognize that you have approached both the ARB and the DGS for assistance
before being directed to the FPPC, and that resolution of your status will be of little use if
it cannot be done within the next six months. Nevertheless, if you wish to challenge the
ARB's designation of your former position in its conflict of interest code, you must
petition the ARB for an amendment to that code, appealing any adverse decision to the
FPPC within 30 days thereafter, as provided at Section 87307.

Your account of the facts suggests, in any event, that the ultimate barrier to your
CMAS contract is classification of your former position as "policy making" under DGS
Rule 62. The FPPC cannot provide you with an authoritative interpretation of this DGS
rule, but you should not assume that re-designation of your position in the ARB conflict
of interest code is necessary or useful to the DGS. We suggest that you contact DGS and
obtain a clear understanding of the requirements of Rule 62 before you attempt to secure
an amendment to the ARB code.

If you have any other questions on this matter, please call me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Luisa Menchaca
Assistant General Counsel

By: Lawrence T. Woodlock
Senior Commission Counsel
